messages.author: aquarat
messages.text: I started working on a logger for Meshcore, specifically for my own device adverts, but I got a bit carried away and landed up creating a web based chat interface. It's not intended for the public to use it to send messages, it's just a nice view into what my node can see. The app also responds to "ping" and "test" messages in the #test channel. It's still very rough. It doesn't make use of any existing Meshcore-project code, the radio interface is a clean implementation in Typescript, which I might open source. Check out the website and LMK if you find it useful.
messages.content_html: I started working on a logger for Meshcore, specifically for my own device adverts, but I got a bit carried away and landed up creating a web based chat interface. It's not intended for the public to use it to send messages, it's just a nice view into what my node can see. The app also responds to "ping" and "test" messages in the #test channel. It's still very rough. It doesn't make use of any existing Meshcore-project code, the radio interface is a clean implementation in Typescript, which I might open source. Check out the website and LMK if you find it useful.
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: aquarat
messages.text: Haha, thanks. It's a real mix of concerns. I need to split stuff up into separate apps/packages.
messages.content_html: Haha, thanks. It's a real mix of concerns. I need to split stuff up into separate apps/packages.

messages.author: Clinton
messages.text: How on earth did you develop your own typescript library from scratch?
messages.content_html: How on earth did you develop your own typescript library from scratch?

messages.author: aquarat
messages.text: I used an LLM (aka AI), with a lot of guidance. I prompted one LLM to scan the Meshcore source code and build up a summary of the serial protocol. I reviewed that, guided it to stuff that was missing that I knew I wanted. Then I took that summary and gave it to another LLM and prompted it to create a library for it. After quite a few iterations the library seemed largely complete, so I then wrote a basic logger app with SQLite and Prisma as the ORM. Tested that, found a few bugs, fixed them one by one. (edited)
messages.content_html: I used an LLM (aka AI), with a lot of guidance. I prompted one LLM to scan the Meshcore source code and build up a summary of the serial protocol. I reviewed that, guided it to stuff that was missing that I knew I wanted. Then I took that summary and gave it to another LLM and prompted it to create a library for it. After quite a few iterations the library seemed largely complete, so I then wrote a basic logger app with SQLite and Prisma as the ORM. Tested that, found a few bugs, fixed them one by one. (edited)

messages.author: aquarat
messages.text: I think it's pretty cool (that this is feasible option now). It's a controversial thing, but ultimately it allowed me to build something reasonable in a short space of time. There is a Meshcore JS library, but it isn't typed and trying to retroactively add types was a bit painful. It also targets browsers, whereas I'm purely interested in a CLI environment. (edited)

messages.author: aquarat
messages.text: Hey, I haven't worked on this in a while. I stopped after I got it to respond to DMs (it does the cryptography in Typescript). But yes, the code is very messy from experimentation, needs serious cleanup.
